After he had finished I noticed that Adolf Hitler, who had received my husband's statements without pertinent remarks, seemed to be a little absentminded, so that I had the impression that my husband's statements had not made any convincing impression." I have offered this affidavit so as to prove that at that time Ribbentrop was still eager to avoid a conflict with Russia. This ends the presentation of the documents on behalf of the Defendant Von Ribbentrop.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Sir David, could you inform us how far you have been able to get with Dr. Thoma in connection with his documents, that is, the Rosenberg documents?

turns.speaker: SIR DAVID MAXWELL-FYFE
turns.text: My Lord, the American delegation, the Soviet, and the French are dealing with Rosenberg.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Perhaps Mr. Dodd can tell us.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: Captain Krieger of our staff, Your Honor, has been in consultation with Dr. Thoma and will continue to be, in an effort to follow the procedures laid out by the Court.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: While on that subject, if I may, I would like to inform the Court that we have concluded our conversations with Dr. Dix, and we are, I think it fair to say, at some differences. I think it would be necessary to have a hearing by the Court on these matters that we do not agree on. However, we have agreed to a considerable number of Schacht items.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, but what I want to insure is that there shall be no delay at the end of Kaltenbrunner's case with reference to Rosenberg's case. And as I understand it, the documents in the Rosenberg case, which it has been suggested we might have to consider, are very numerous; and the sooner the Tribunal gets to them the better.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: We shall be available at all times to talk with Dr. Thoma and move right along-in the evening if he cares to do it.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: It might possibly be desirable, it seems to me, to have the documents which have been translated presented to the Tribunal before the others; I mean to say not have them all together, because there are, no doubt, various volumes.

turns.speaker: MR. DODD
turns.text: There are three so far; I understand there will be more. But we will press it and continue to talk with Dr. Thoma, and just as soon as possible on the first book we will be prepared to come before the Court for a hearing.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, Dr. Thoma, wouldn't the best thing be for you to submit the volumes which have been translated to the Court so that they can consider them beforehand as we did with Dr. Horn's books?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Yes, My Lord, that is possible. The documents have already been processed. With reference to my Document Books Number 2 and 3, I have discussed them with Captain Krieger, in Room 216, and we came to an agreement.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Yes, well you could specify that agreement in the books. I suppose you could show which documents you were prepared to withdraw.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, then, as soon as possible you will let the General Secretary have those books, showing the agreement which you have made with Captain Krieger; is that right?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: But I do want to point out that I have come to an agreement with Captain Krieger, in Room 216, only with reference to Books 2 and 3 and that refers only to the Einsatzstab and the Reich Minister for the Occupied Eastern Territories. I have not yet come to an agreement regarding the philosophy and writings of Rosenberg, but I shall do that in due course.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: No; one-is that in Book 1?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Yes.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Well, if you are unable to come to an agreement, you can specify that, and we will consider those matters. Possibly you could take some time with Captain Krieger-take time off from Court-in order to come to an agreement with reference to Book 1 and with reference to the other books. How many more books have you got?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: All together four document books.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Four more?

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: All together four document books.

turns.speaker: THE PRESIDENT
turns.text: Oh yes, I see. So there is only one more to be translated.

turns.speaker: DR. THOMA
turns.text: Yes. [The Tribunal adjourned until 11 April 1946 at 1000 hours]
